content.text: A running agent occupies a feedback loop: it calls a model, dispatches tool calls, accumulates results, and loops. Without an interrupt mechanism, the only way to stop it is to kill the process — which leaves resources allocated, side effects half-applied, and context unrecoverable. Structured interruption gives humans a clean way to intervene at any point in an agent's lifecycle.

attrs.alt: Agent interrupt signal types and state transitions

content.text: Agent loops do not have natural preemption points. A model call may take 10–30 seconds; a tool call could run for minutes. Naive approaches fail in predictable ways:
— leaves sockets open, partial writes on disk, database transactions uncommitted.
— the flag is only checked between iterations; a slow model call or tool blocks checking indefinitely.
— aborts during model streaming, which the SDK may not handle gracefully.
The correct approach is cooperative interruption: the agent checks a cancellation token at defined checkpoints, and external callers signal through that token.
Abandon current iteration; do not start new tool calls
Finish the current step, then suspend
Complete in-flight tool call; serialize state; halt
Inject new objective into conversation; continue loop
Add human turn to message list; resubmit to model
Move item to head of work queue; switch focus
The foundational primitive is a cancellation token: an object shared between the interrupt sender and the agent loop. asyncio provides this natively via
The token is created by the caller and passed into the agent task. The caller retains a reference and can signal at any time:

async def demo():
    token = InterruptToken()

    # Launch agent as a background task
    agent_task = asyncio.create_task(
        run_interruptible_agent(
            system="You are a helpful assistant.",
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Analyse this dataset..."}],
            tools=MY_TOOLS,
            token=token,
        )
    )

    # After 10 seconds, pause the agent
    await asyncio.sleep(10)
    print("Pausing agent...")
    token.pause()

    # After 5 more seconds, inject a message and resume
    await asyncio.sleep(5)
    token.inject("Focus only on the revenue columns.")
    token.resume()

    # After 20 more seconds, cancel entirely
    await asyncio.sleep(20)
    print("Cancelling agent...")
    token.cancel()

    try:
        result = await agent_task
        print("Agent completed:", result)
    except AgentInterrupted as e:
        print("Agent was interrupted:", e)
Model streaming complicates cancellation: the SDK is mid-HTTP-response and cannot be cleanly aborted without closing the connection. Two options:
— don't interrupt during streaming; check the token immediately after the stream completes. For most agents, stream latency (10–30 s) is tolerable.
— call the streaming API and break out of the iterator when the cancel token is set. Discard the partial response and re-enter the loop, which will immediately see the cancel flag.

# Streaming with early-exit on cancel
def model_call_with_cancel(messages, tools, token: InterruptToken) -> list:
    content_blocks = []
    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=4096,
        messages=messages,
        tools=tools,
    ) as stream:
        for event in stream:
            if token.is_cancelled:
                # Close the stream early; content_blocks is partial
                return None   # caller interprets None as cancelled
            # accumulate events into content_blocks
            # (simplified — real SDK provides .get_final_message())
    return content_blocks
Human wants to inspect state before aborting
Agent may hold locks while paused
Clear user intent to stop; no state needed
In-flight tool calls may leave partial side effects
Goal changed but work so far is still useful
Model may ignore redirect if context is large
Last resort — agent is hung and unresponsive
Always prefer CANCEL over process kill. The cancel token gives the agent a chance to flush logs, release locks, and record a checkpoint. SIGTERM should only follow after a cancel timeout.
Log every interrupt event with enough context to reconstruct what the agent was doing:

import json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, UTC

INTERRUPT_LOG = Path("interrupt_events.jsonl")

def log_interrupt(
    agent_id: str,
    signal: str,
    iteration: int,
    last_tool: str | None = None,
    extra: dict | None = None,
):
    record = {
        "ts": datetime.now(UTC).isoformat(),
        "agent_id": agent_id,
        "signal": signal,
        "iteration": iteration,
        "last_tool": last_tool,
        **(extra or {}),
    }
    with INTERRUPT_LOG.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")

When agents spawn sub-agents, interrupt signals must propagate down the tree. An orchestrator receiving a CANCEL must forward it to every active sub-agent it manages, then wait for them to terminate before considering itself done:
The key invariant: when the orchestrator's token is cancelled, every child token must also be cancelled before the orchestrator raises
. Partial cancellation — where some sub-agents continue running after the parent is cancelled — is a resource leak and a consistency risk.
An agent that can be interrupted partway through a sequence of tool calls must handle the case where some calls completed and others did not. Design tool calls to be idempotent where possible:
instead of insert + update sequences — a single idempotent operation is safe to retry after interruption.
— record 'will send email to X' to a log before sending. On resume, check whether the send already occurred.
— mark each item as PENDING → IN_PROGRESS → DONE. On resume, skip DONE items and restart from the oldest IN_PROGRESS.

import asyncio
import pytest

async def slow_tool(name: str, params: dict) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(5)   # simulate slow tool
    return f"{name} completed"

async def test_cancel_before_tool():
    token = InterruptToken()
    token.cancel()   # cancel immediately

    with pytest.raises(AgentInterrupted):
        await run_interruptible_agent(
            system="Test",
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Run a slow task"}],
            tools=[{"name": "slow_tool", "description": "...", "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {}}}],
            token=token,
        )

async def test_pause_and_resume():
    token = InterruptToken()
    token.pause()   # start paused

    task = asyncio.create_task(
        run_interruptible_agent(
            system="Test",
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}],
            tools=[],
            token=token,
        )
    )

    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # let task reach pause checkpoint
    assert not task.done()   # still blocked

    token.resume()
    result = await asyncio.wait_for(task, timeout=30)
    assert result   # completed after resume

Structured agent interruption requires a shared cancellation token checked at well-defined checkpoints: before the model call, after the model call returns, and before each tool call. Support at minimum CANCEL (immediate stop) and PAUSE/RESUME (temporary suspension). Expose these signals via HTTP endpoints so operators can interrupt without direct process access. Never rely on killing the process as a primary mechanism — it bypasses cleanup and leaves the system in an unknown state.
